segments.text: Hi everyone, my name is Kang and I'm a
product manager at Google Cloud working
on zerative media. In this video, I
would like to give you an introductions
about generative media which are the AI
models that allows you to generate
images, videos and audios from a text
prompt. In Google Cloud, we offer four
gen media models. Imagine for image
generations, VO for video generations,
chirp for speech generations, and LIA
for music
generations. For each model, I'll go
through the popular use cases that many
developers are leveraging them for. Then
I'll talk about the technical details on
how you can get started with using this
model. Now, let's dive into it. We'll
start with imagen.
The most basic use case that you can use
imagen for is to generate highquality
images from a text prompt. It can
generate many different kinds of images
like photorealistic images which would
be very difficult and costly to capture
in real life such as this drawn
photo or this photo of a
hummingbird. Imagine can also do macro
photography and creating very realistic
and artistic lighting condition.
Imagine can also generate science
fiction images or images of different
art
styles. Beyond generating images from
text prompts, imagine can do image
editing as well. It can remove objects
from the image so that it can turn this
photo into a beautiful photo of the you.
It can remove reflections on windows as
well to make a picture perfect
photo. Besides object removal, imagine
can also insert objects into images or
extend an image and fill in the missing
areas. These features can be used to
correct photos taken at a bad angle, for
example. Imagine can even completely
replace the background of the photo,
making them more interesting.
It also can stylize your image into many
different art styles including oil
painting, 3D art, and many
more. The background replacement
features is actually quite useful in
e-commerce as well because it can
generate different backgrounds for your
product
image. It works for a wide range of
products from small items like this
perfume bottle to big items like
appliance or furniture.
So that was a highlight of features that
imagine offers. Now let me show you how
to use imagen in code. Imagine has a
restful API which is available through
Google cloud. You can call the rest API
directly from your applications or you
can use one of our gener SDK to make it
easier. Next let me show you how to use
imagen with the Python SDK.
You start with installing the Google
gener SDK using
pip. Then you'll need to authenticate
your environment. If you are using
Google Collab, you can use it off module
to do it
quickly. If you're using a local Python
environment, you can use a good Zcloud
CLI. Run this command and authenticate
with your user
credential. Then you need to create a
Zenai client.
Next, you define the text prompt that
you want to use. Then choose an imagen
model. Here we choose imagen 3 generate
002 which is the latest and highest
quality imagine model as of May
2025 and just call generate images. Wait
a few seconds for it to
generate and then save the generated
image in a
file. Very easy, isn't it? There are
several parameters that you can use to
customize our image generation
request. For example, you can choose how
many images you want to generate in a
single request. You can choose any value
between one and four. And if you choose
to generate more than one image, you
will get multiple variations and pick
the one that you like the most to
use. You can choose the aspect ratio for
the generated images as well.
So that was image generations. Now let's
talk about image
editing. To edit an image like this with
imagen basically you need to define a
mask which tells which part of the image
that you wanted to
edit. If you want to remove the objects
from the image then the mask is all you
need. But if you want to insert objects
into the image, then you'll need a
prompt to specify what to
insert. The Python code to do image
editing with IMAGEN is very similar to
the image generations codes that you saw
earlier with a few
changes. First, you need to import a few
more types from the Genai
SDK. Then you define the original
image and the mask image.
Then you call the edit image
functions. You specify the editing mode
to use here. For object removal, you
don't need to write a
prompt. But to insert an object, you
need to specify what you want to insert
in the
prompt. And finally, you save the output
image to a file just like what you did
earlier with image
generations. Very easy, isn't it? Please
also check out the Imagen Collab
notebooks in the video description for
more examples of how you can use Imagen
to generate and edit
images. Now let's move over to VO, our
video generation
[Music]
model. V2 creates highquality videos
covering a wide range of topics and
styles. In fact, when we compared to
other video generations models, people
consistently rated V2 as producing
better results. VO's most basic features
is to allow you to generate videos from
text prompts. As in May 2025, you can
generate videos of up to 8
seconds. Just like
imaginate many different kinds of videos
from photorealistic, science fictions,
animations, and many more.
You can have more control over the
output video by using an image as the
initial frame and writing a prompt to
turn it into a
video because an image is faster than
zing a video. This approach allows you
to quickly iterate to get the visual
that you want before turning them into a
video. You can also specify different
camera movements such as panning left
and right or pushing in to have more
control over the output
video. One interesting use case of
imagetovideo capability is to turn a
static banner ad like this popcorn
ad into an engaging short video ad like
this
one. V API is also available through the
Googlei SDK and let me show you how to
use it in Python.
You start with installing the same
Google geniip pit
package. Then import the geni module and

we'll use a model called v2.0 generate
001 for text to video generations.
Similar to text to image generations
with I imagine we define the
prompt the aspects
ratio the number of variations we want
to
create and the length of each

functions you will get back a long
running operations which you will need
to pull it periodically and wait until

completed to download the video.
It will take about 20 seconds to
generate a 8-second
video. Once it has completed, you can
save the generated
video. If you want to specify an image
as the initial frame for the generated
video, you can do it by defining the
image and then pass it to the generate
video function. The other parameters
stay the same.
Okay, so that's all you need to generate

Python
SDK. Next, let's talk about CHF, our
latest speech generations
[Music]
model. Text to speech technology has

latest text to speech model really

So, why don't we just let Chirp

this, we support 30 different languages.
30. It's kind of crazy. We're talking

goes on. Imagine reaching a global

We even support different accents within

has you covered. We're versatile,
natural, and ready for anything. Give us

being human, of course. Yep. We're

What do you think? I think Ch 3 sounds

Next, let me show you the code to Z
speech from
text. In order to use chirp in Python,

and the config of the output audio such

That was all you need to generate

tree. Lastly, let's talk about LIA, our
latest model for music
[Music]
generations. The main thing LIA does is
to create instrumental music based on a
single text
description. You give it a prompt
describing the mood, gen, and
instruments that you want, and Lydia
will generate the music. We're not doing

As you can tell, the range of styles is
pretty broad, even within the
instrumental space. These are just
examples dated from text prompts and not
from pre-recorded music at
all. You can use LIA for many different
use cases. For example, a game developer
may need to create unique background
music for different levels. They can use
LRA to quickly generate tracks that
perfectly match the mood and setting of
each level, saving them time and money
on custom
compositions. Podcasters could use LRA
to create intro and outro music or even
background music for specific segments.
They could easily generate royaltyfree
music following the podcast theme and
tone.
Meditation apps could use LIA to create
personalized music experience for each
of their users. They can customize the
prompts to evoke specific emotions or
memories. Okay, so far I've shown you
how to use each Z media model
individually, but actually you can do
more than
that. I'll show you a workflow to
combine all four models together to
create longer videos for many different
use cases. video ads, visual
storytelling, short movies, you name
it. The typical workflow would start
with writing a storyboard for your e
video. You can use Gemini to have you
brainstorming or writing the whole
storyboard based on your
idea. Here's my storyboard for a short
ad video for an imaginary coffee brand
which consists of six different scenes.
Then you can use imagine to generate the
first frame for each
scene. You can iterate on it until you
get the visual that you
like. After that, you use the VO image
to video feature to generate a short
clip for each
scene. Then you use Chirp and Lia to add
music and narrations to the video.
Now, let's take a look at the premium
coffee video ad that I created using
this workflow. Premium coffee selects
only the top 1% of fair trade Arabica
beans. Slow roasted, perfectly
brewed, releasing notes of chocolate and
spice. Premium coffee. The taste of true

I hope that you like the video. To

media models
today. Imagin for generating
images, VO for generating

music. You can find the collab notebook

Thank you for watching.
